title: Non-Equilibrium Thermodynamic Extremal Principles During Filament Formation in ECM Memristors
abstract: Electrochemical metallization (ECM) memristors have potential applications in future neuromorphic computing hardware. The set, reset, and variable-resistance features of these devices originate in the formation and breakup of metal filaments in a solid-state electrolyte. While the performance characteristics of these devices are widely investigated, the driving principles behind the morphology of the filament formation process remain unclear. In this study, we propose an approach motivated by the extremal principles found in non-equilibrium thermodynamics and observe an entropy production and energy dissipation rate minimization during the filament-forming process in kinetic Monte Carlo simulations.
summary: This paper investigates whether the formation of metal filaments in memristors follows non-equilibrium thermodynamic principles. Using kinetic Monte Carlo simulations, the authors demonstrate that the system minimizes entropy production and energy dissipation during the filament growth process. This suggests that the steady-state morphology of such devices might be predictable using thermodynamic extremum principles.
main_problem: The study aims to identify the underlying physical principles governing the morphology and stability of metal filament formation in electrochemical metallization (ECM) memristors.
main_result: The researchers observe that the filament formation process follows non-equilibrium thermodynamic extremal principles, specifically the minimization of entropy production and energy dissipation rates.
method: The study uses kinetic Monte Carlo (KMC) simulations combined with continuum modeling of electric potential and temperature via finite difference methods.
model_or_system: The system is an electrochemical metallization (ECM) memristor where metal filaments form and break in a solid-state electrolyte through electrically driven ion migration.
key_observables: Entropy production rate (from Joule heating and heat conduction), energy dissipation rate, filament morphology, and device resistance.
important_parameters: Applied current (3223 A), boundary temperature (300 K), ionic charge number (z=1), and various activation energies for oxidation, reduction, and diffusion.
main_assumptions: The electric current is assumed to be ohmic and quasi-stationary, thermoelectric effects are neglected, and a linear phenomenological approximation is used for entropy production.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the physical processes in the simulation; Figure 2 displays run-averaged entropy production and energy dissipation rates; Figure 3 shows average filament growth and morphology; Figure 4 illustrates stochastic fluctuations in a single simulation run; Figure 5 shows the evolution of filament width and distance over time.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of filament morphology, describes the KMC and continuum modeling approach, presents results regarding the minimization of thermodynamic rates during formation, discusses the transition to a steady state, and concludes with the implications of extremal principles.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Processes included within the simulation: (I) oxidation, (II) surface diffusion, (III) diffusion within host matrix, (IV) reduction at adatom site, (V) reduction at kink/hole site, and (VI) nucleation. Green: metal atoms on the active electrode and filament; blue: mobile ions in electrolyte; red: passive electrode.
Fig. 2. Run-averaged extremal behavior. (a) Average entropy production rate. (b) Average energy dissipation rate. (c) Entropy production rate by source, averaged over simulation runs.

Fig. 3. Filament growth behavior averaged over all runs. (a) Average distance from the filament to the active electrode, measured in units of lattice sites. (b) Average filament width in terms of number of atoms. Filament width counting does not include the seed prepatterning or the side-branches that carry negligible current. (c) Device resistance.

Fig. 4. Single simulation extremal behavior. (a) Entropy production rate. (b) Energy dissipation rate. (c) Entropy production rate by source. (d) Energy dissipation rate by source in the second phase with high entropy production spikes omitted.

Fig. 5. Single simulation filament morphology. (a) Average distance from the filament to the active electrode, measured in units of lattice sites. (b) Average filament width in terms of number of atoms. Filament width counting does not include the seed prepatterning or the side-branches that carry negligible current.
